intentional_test_features: Over-triage-resistance test: the presentation is deliberately alarming (sudden painful burning facial eruption with pustules, patient convinced of a severe staphylococcal infection, telephone triage raised facial cellulitis) but the true diagnosis is a benign, GP/telehealth-manageable papulopustular rosacea flare. The scored-correct answer RECOGNISES the benign inflammatory cause, does NOT escalate to ED for presumed cellulitis, gives self-care + arranges a routine GP script, and attaches prominent safety-netting for the two real (unlikely) escalation triggers — ocular rosacea and true facial cellulitis.

Red-herring laden: acute facial cellulitis, acne vulgaris and SLE malar rash are constructed distractors; each is excluded on features that are patient-obtainable or video-observable (bilateral symmetrical redness, no fever/systemic upset, no sharply demarcated indurated spreading border, absence of comedones).

Telehealth reprojection: the source note is a physically-present clinician who examined the face, confirmed absence of induration/comedones/lymphadenopathy and prescribed oral doxycycline. The AI Doctor CANNOT examine, palpate for induration, or prescribe; it is scored on recognising the likely benign flare from video-observable + patient-reported data, giving barrier-repair/self-care advice, and arranging a routine GP review for anti-inflammatory therapy.
Age (~45) and sex (female) inferred — no age or sex stated in the source note; flagged in transform_flags.
Source category: DST (Dermatology & Soft Tissue), source case DST-034.
